entries.summary: The former name of Oslo, Norway; the city where the novel is set.
A baker or bread shop mentioned in the boarding-house advertisement; it signals the ordinary city life around the narrator.
An official notice from the government office overseeing lighthouses; here it is just part of the printed matter on the wall.
A Norwegian newspaper; here it is one of the old papers pasted on the wall.
A recurring idealized woman in Hunger, partly romantic fantasy and partly projection of the narrator's desires.
A fanciful invented name the narrator uses in one of his feverish stories and self-dramatizing fantasies.
A reference to Saint Olaf, a major Norwegian historical and religious figure; in context it points to a named place or institution.
Knut Hamsun, the Norwegian author of Hunger.
